serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of dry chicken and mushroom noodles is approximately 250 g, providing around 278 kcal, 16.8 g protein, 9.3 g fat, and 31.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken and Mushroom Noodles (Dry) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken and Mushroom Noodles (Dry), record F05060023. Iron value reported as -1 in source, indicating data not available in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — egg noodles (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry-tossed chicken and mushroom egg noodles. GI of 47 (low) is estimated from boiled egg noodles as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (47) × available carbohydrate per serving (250 g × 12.7 g/100 g = 31.75 g) / 100 = 14.9; however, dry-tossed preparation with oyster sauce gravy and protein/fibre content moderates glycaemic response, and a conservative GL of 7.5 per 100 g equivalent portion is used. GL per 250 g serving = approximately 15.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 428 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium seasoning, as the 428 mg sodium per 100 g contributes substantially toward the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (12.7 g/100 g) from refined egg noodles with low GI (~47) partially offset by protein (6.7 g) and dietary fibre (1.7 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes can include this dish in a controlled meal plan by limiting serving size to 150–200 g, choosing wholegrain noodle variants where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (428 mg/100 g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (~0.8 g/100 g) and total fat is moderate (3.7 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, the high sodium content is the dominant risk factor; patients should request reduced-sodium preparation, limit portion to 150–200 g, and balance the meal with potassium-rich foods such as leafy greens to support vascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken and Mushroom Noodles (Dry) is a popular Singaporean hawker and food court dish consisting of boiled egg noodles tossed dry (without soup broth) with shredded chicken, sliced mushrooms, and green vegetables such as choy sum or kai lan, finished with an oyster sauce-based gravy. The noodles are springy and slightly chewy, the chicken is tender and mildly savoury, and the mushrooms contribute an earthy umami depth. The dish is served warm and is typically consumed as a complete one-dish meal at lunch or dinner. Per 100 g, it provides 111 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 3.7 g fat, 12.7 g carbohydrate, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and 428 mg sodium. The moderate protein and fibre content, combined with a low estimated glycaemic index (~47 from egg noodle analogue), makes it a reasonable choice for glycaemic management when portion-controlled. However, the sodium content at 428 mg/100 g is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to chicken and oyster sauce, and contains gluten (egg noodles), egg, soy, and shellfish allergens. It is commonly found in Singaporean hawker centres, food courts, and coffee shops, and is known in Chinese as 干捞鸡肉蘑菇面 and in Malay as Mi Ayam Cendawan (Kering).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F05060023) with confidence 0.95. The dish is a canonical Singaporean hawker item with an exact HPB record. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (boiled egg noodles, University of Sydney GI Database) and documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: iron_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source record F05060023 reports iron as -1 (data not available), indicating this micronutrient was not measured or reported for this specific dish preparation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and mineral data for this prepared noodle dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 0.8 g/100 g is estimated from the fat composition of egg noodles and chicken (low saturated fat profile) consistent with USDA FoodData Central analogues; this is an estimate and not directly reported by HPB. The input record incorrectly tagged this dish as 'western' cuisine and included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' dietary tags; these have been corrected — the dish is Singaporean cuisine and is not vegetarian or vegan due to chicken and oyster sauce content. Halal status is not confirmed as oyster sauce sourcing varies; is_halal set to false pending verification. GI glycemic load per serving note: GL per 250 g serving is approximately 15 (moderate); the per-100 g GL figure of 7.5 is provided for consistency with the per_100g nutrient framework.
